coredata.dc:title: Efficient CQDs/Cu2S @Ti-TPA-MOF heterojunction incorporation: A visible-light-photocatalytic composite with extended separated charges lifetime

coredata.dc:description: A sustainable strategy to tackle water pollution involves using innovative composites based on MOFs for photodecomposing antibiotic contaminants. A core-shell structure was synthesized using a solvothermal method, combining Cu2S, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and Ti-TPA-MOF, resulting in a material with excellent visible-light capture capabilities. Spectroscopic (XRD, XPS, BET, DRS, DLS, ESR, and PL), electrochemical (photocurrent), and microscopic (TEM) analyses were performed to characterize the photocatalyst. Adding 20 mg of Cu2S and CQDs greatly improved gentamicin photodegradation compared to other prepared photocatalysts. This was due to enhanced electron conductivity, increased photocurrent density, a narrower energy gap, and a longer charge separation lifetime. The heterojunction composites at 150 mg/l showed a 2–6 times increase in efficiency after 90 min, surpassing the pure materials. The outcomes showed evidence of pseudo-first-order kinetics, photocatalytic activity, and excitation by visible light in the photodegradation. The stability of the photocatalytic process is evident after five cycles.

head.ce:title: Efficient CQDs/Cu2S @Ti-TPA-MOF heterojunction incorporation: A visible-light-photocatalytic composite with extended separated charges lifetime

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sustainable strategy to tackle water pollution involves using innovative composites based on MOFs for photodecomposing antibiotic contaminants. A core-shell structure was synthesized using a solvothermal method, combining Cu2S, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and Ti-TPA-MOF, resulting in a material with excellent visible-light capture capabilities. Spectroscopic (XRD, XPS, BET, DRS, DLS, ESR, and PL), electrochemical (photocurrent), and microscopic (TEM) analyses were performed to characterize the photocatalyst. Adding 20 mg of Cu2S and CQDs greatly improved gentamicin photodegradation compared to other prepared photocatalysts. This was due to enhanced electron conductivity, increased photocurrent density, a narrower energy gap, and a longer charge separation lifetime. The heterojunction composites at 150 mg/l showed a 2–6 times increase in efficiency after 90 min, surpassing the pure materials. The outcomes showed evidence of pseudo-first-order kinetics, photocatalytic activity, and excitation by visible light in the photodegradation. The stability of the photocatalytic process is evident after five cycles.

ce:section.ce:para: Persistent organic contaminants in water from industrial or urban sources and antibiotic use in various sectors threaten human health and ecosystems. Emphasizing the importance of executing a cost-efficient plan is necessary to safeguard the sustainability of aquatic habitats (Abbassian and Rahmani, 2024; Alamgholiloo et al., 2021; H. Wang et al., 2016). Semiconductor photocatalysis converts organic pollutants into CO2 and H2O using visible light, offering a sustainable solution for environmental remediation (Abbassian and Rahmani, 2024; Odling et al., 2018; Truong et al., 2020). Photocatalytic processes are effective in eliminating contaminants due to their economic feasibility (Akpan and Hameed, 2009; Al-Nuaim et al., 2023; M.-H. Fan et al., 2023; Kaur et al., 2020; Sarafraz et al., 2020; Sharma et al., 2021; D. Zhu and Zhou, 2019). Traditional semiconductor photocatalysts have constraints like limited surface area, inadequate sensitivity to visible light, and elevated rates of (e-)-(h+) pairs recombination (Han et al., 2020; Yunfang Huang et al., 2017; X. Liu et al., 2024; Yu et al., 2021).

Lately, there have been notable progressions in photocatalytic metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), recognized for their optical and electronic properties, large surface area, and capacity to modify crystallinity. MOFs' porous structure helps absorb pollutants efficiently, and their active sites produce reactive species (Zhongwu Li et al., 2021; J. Lin et al., 2020; Muelas-Ramos, Sampaio et al., 2021; J. Zhu et al., 2018). However, efforts are being made to improve the limitations of MOFs as photocatalysts (Castellanos et al., 2019; Muelas-Ramos et al., 2021; K. Yue et al., 2021). The use of MOFs like Ti-TPA-MOF is limited because of the lack of a visible-light band gap and unstable excited electrons. Developing heterojunction composites with sulfide nanoparticles and quantum dots in core-shell structures is a practical and eco-friendly approach. Integrating MOFs and NPs retains their unique properties and creates synergistic effects, making them ideal for use as photocatalysts in various sectors (S. Jin et al., 2013; S. Liu et al., 2014; Y. Yang et al., 2022; Zeng et al., 2016; C. Zhao et al., 2021; Z. Zhao et al., 2019).

The fusion of quantum dots (QDs) and MOFs shows great promise for advancing technology and science. This combination can revolutionize various fields by improving stability and adding new functionalities to materials. It also helps prevent quantum dots from collapsing, leading to exciting new applications (Aguilera-Sigalat and Bradshaw, 2016; M. Chen et al., 2020; Y. Si et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2015). QDs-MOF materials offer a promising solution due to their well-structured pores and large surface area, which prevent QDs aggregation and improve electron transport, leading to enhanced performance. Nanosized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are eco-friendly, non-toxic, and can modify luminescence wavelengths. They have efficient light absorption, high-energy light emission, excellent electron conductivity, and are easily functionalized, making them ideal for catalytic processes. Quantum dots in CQDs and MOF structures can act as electron donors or acceptors, enhancing visible light capture and photocatalytic activity by efficiently separating electrons and holes and improving their transfer (Aguilera-Sigalat and Bradshaw, 2016; Chaudhary et al., 2014; Jung et al., 2022; R. Lin et al., 2018; Qin et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2020).

A new composite, S-MIL-101(Fe)@CQDs, was successfully produced through solvothermal synthesis and annealing treatment, showing promise in removing Cr6+ and dyes from wastewater. By adding CQDs to the MOF material, the electronic properties are improved, leading to better photocatalytic performance (Yuwen Huang et al., 2023). A one-step hydrothermal method was employed to create composite photocatalysts of CQDs@MIL-100 (Fe). These photocatalysts exhibited exceptional effectiveness in eliminating NO under visible-light conditions. CQDs incorporation with MIL-100 (Fe) through C-O bonds improved sunlight absorption and charge utilization, effectively converting NO to NO−3 (X. Fan et al., 2021). Heterostructures of MoS2 QDs/NH2-MIL-125 were procreated via adorning MoS2 quantum dots onto NH2-MIL-125 using a straightforward method. The photocatalytic performance of heterostructures composed of MoS2 and NH2-MIL-125 surpasses that of pure materials because of the creation of heterojunctions, which enhances the segregation of charge carriers. The photoluminescence findings confirm a decrease in the recombination process, leading to an improved level of photocatalytic efficiency (H. Li et al., 2019).

The objective of this endeavor is centered around harnessing the potential of a heterojunction composite, which integrates distinct materials, each possessing distinct capabilities, to achieve the intended result. The solvothermal method was utilized to prepare core-shell structured composites consisting of Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF, which were subsequently adorned with CQDs. The CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF heterojunction composite is anticipated to be greatly influenced by the presence of CQDs, as they play crucial roles in tasks such as capturing visible light and aiding in the conduction of electrons. They effectively function as electron collectors, playing a vital role in the overall process. The Cu2S nanoparticles assume a critical function in the band gap reduction and the inhibition of (e-)-(h+) pairs recombination, showcasing its essential role in this context. The outstanding porosity of Ti-TPA-MOF enables it to effectively absorb pollutant molecules, assuming a critical role in minimizing their impact on the environment. The inclusion of Cu2S into the MOF framework, coupled with the introduction of CQDs, is expected to bolster the effectiveness of the composites in producing reactive species for the decomposition of contaminants. This is attributed to the unique optical features of each nanoparticle, which are expected to prolong the lifetime of separated charges and enhance the overall efficiency of the heterojunction composites in photodegradation processes.

Cu2S nanoparticles were generated in bulk using the hydrothermal approach. The procedure involved the dissolution of 0.5 g of Cu (NO3)2·3 H2O (Merck, purity ≥99.0 %) in 20 ml of double distilled water. The mixing procedure occurred at the standard ambient temperature and was finished in a span of 15 minutes. The mixture was alkalized and maintained at a pH of 10 by adding 25 % ammonium hydroxide continuously while mixing. The process continued by gradually adding a 20 ml volume of an aqueous solution involving 0.5 g of thiourea (Merck, purity ≥99.0 %). This mixture was then blended for 30 min. Afterward, the mixture was relocated to an autoclave lined with Teflon and carefully positioned inside a heated oven. It was slowly heated to 160 °C over 120 min to facilitate the desired reaction. Once the reaction is finished, the autoclave is removed from the oven and left to cool down slowly until it reaches room temperature. Subsequently, the precipitate is gathered, rinsed, and dried at 60 °C.

By following the synthetic route provided, the core-shell composites were successfully fabricated. For 15 min, 2.0 ml of dried methanol (Merck, ≥99.0 %) was used to disperse 20 mg of Cu2S in the standard procedure. The blend that ensued was subsequently introduced into a moisture-deprived DMF (Merck, ≥99.0 %) solution (15.0 ml), which had already been combined with 0.55 g of terephthalic acid (Tondgooyan, ≥ 99 %). The mixture was then ultrasonically treated for 20 min. Following that, the admixtures underwent a vigorous stirring process for 20 min, during which 0.5 ml of titanium isopropoxide (DAE JUNG, ≥98 %) was gradually and carefully injected drop by drop. Subsequently, the admixture was moved into a Teflon-coated steel autoclave and placed at 150 °C for 20 h. The entire process was carried out within a glove box under nitrogen. The collected powder was obtained using a centrifuge, while the cooling was achieved under ambient conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Incorporating of Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF composite with CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this stage, the dispersed composite was mixed with 20 ml of distilled water containing 1 g of citric acid (Merck, ≥99.0 %) and the dissolved PVP. The blend was gently stirred while adding 1 ml of ethylene diamine (Merck, ≥99.0 %), resulting in the solution turning yellow within 30 min. To prevent photo-corrosion, control the particle growth, and hinder the aggregation of QDs, the solution was enriched with Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP). Subsequently, the admixture was relocated to an autoclave and heated at 180°C for 120 min. Once cooling, the precipitated sample was subjected to a thorough wash and subsequently dried at 50°C. Fig. 1 showcases the various stages of synthesis.

The crystal structure was analyzed using X-ray diffraction with a PHILIPS PW1730 X-ray diffractometer from the Netherlands, scanning from 50 to 800(2θ). The diffuse reflectance spectrum (DRS) was estimated using the UV–visible near-infrared absorption spectrometer SCINCO S_4100 from South Korea. The PL spectra were analyzed at room temperature utilizing an Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (G9800A, America). The catalysts' surface chemical components were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, SPECS Phobos 150, Germany). The analysis dynamic light scattering (DLS) was carried using a Bettersize Nanoptic (plus 90, France) instrument. The German Zeiss EM900 was used to study MOF samples' morphology through TEM at 20°C with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The free radicals produced by the reaction with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide DMPO were captured by an ESR spectrometer (Bruker E300, German). An elemental total organic carbon analyzer a Liqui TOC (Germany) was employed for the TOC measurements. The BELSORP mini II equipment was used to obtain N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms at 77 K, and the specific surface area was determined using the BET method.

The current consideration intended to assess the effectiveness and efficiency of employing heterostructure photocatalysts by investigating the degradation of gentamicin as a model in a photoreactor operating in a water-based environment under visible-light conditions. In a 1000 ml solution, Cu2S nanoparticles, pure Ti-TPA-MOF, and its based composites were dispersed as photocatalysts with 150 mg. The light intensity used for excitation was 7.83 mW/cm2, measured with an HPCS-6500 spectroradiometer over an area of 18.39 cm2. To boost the productivity of the photoreaction, a jacketed lamp with a power of 60 watts and a wavelength exceeding 400 nanometers was positioned near the photocatalyst. The lamp operated at 5.7 A and 13.5 V. The experiment mixture that had been prepared was subsequently transferred into a photoreactor with a jacket to carry out the reaction under room temperature conditions. Before irradiation, the photocatalytic solution was stirred for 60 min without light to reach absorption and desorption equilibrium. The experiment solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min to eliminate the photocatalyst. Following the removal process, the resulting suspension (5 ml) was examined employing UV–vis absorption spectroscopy at 400 nm during various time intervals. An examination was conducted on the photocatalytic efficiency of CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF when exposed to a mixture of 25 mg/l gentamicin and various pollutants including azithromycin, acetaminophen, and phenol at a concentration of 1 mg/l. Moreover, the heterojunction composites were re-tested to determine their stability and recyclability under visible-light exposure. Following each trial, the photocatalyst was isolated via centrifuging at 6000 rpm and underwent three washing cycles with demineralized water and methanol to remove any remaining gentamicin.

The determination of whether the nanoparticles were amorphous or crystalline was carried out using the XRD technique, as illustrated in Fig. 2(a). The successful synthesis can be easily verified by examining the XRD patterns of both the original Ti-MOF (with clear peaks observed at 6.53°, 9.52°, 11.56°, 16.47°, 17.84°, and 19.52° (Abbassian and Rahmani, 2024)) and the composite material consisting of CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TAP-MOF. The main peaks observed at 6.53, 9.52, and 11.56° represent the crystal planes (101), (200), and (221) in the Ti-TPA-MOF sample, confirming the successful synthesis of the material (Song et al., 2021). The emergence of extra peaks in the XRD spectrum of CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF was ascribed to Cu2S nanoparticles at various positions, notably at 25.44°, 28.64°, and 36. 44°. Upon synthesis, the Cu2S sample displays three distinct peaks at 25.44°, 28.64°, and 36.44°, corresponding to the (304), (314), and (052) planes of Cu2S. These peaks closely align with the data provided in literature (JCPDS card no.02–1284), indicating a high degree of agreement in the crystal structure analysis (L. Jin et al., 2019; Mousavi-Kamazani et al., 2013). Additionally, the presence of strong peaks indicates the crystalline structure of the core-shell composites within the heterojunction. Despite incorporating CQDs into the heterojunction composite, it is difficult to observe a distinct peak due to the overlapping main peak of CQDs and the peak of Cu2S at a specific angle of 25°. The effectiveness of composite production has been convincingly showcased by the alignment of peaks in the XRD diagram for each sample, along with the attendance of core-shell structures and heterostructure composites. The even dispersion of nanoparticles within the Ti-MOF and the lack of extra peaks in the XRD spectrum point toward the high purity of the synthesized nanoparticles. The crystallinity percentage was calculated by deconvoluting the diffractogram using OriginPro software and applying the specified equation (Abbassian and Rahmani, 2024; Gómez-Avilés et al., 2020).
(1)

Area of crystalline peaks

Through calculations, the crystallinity percentages of the novel heterojunction composites, Ti-MOF, Cu2S, and Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF, were found to be 58.6 %, 25.6 %, and 31.3 %, respectively. In theory, enhancing the crystallinity not only boosts the movement of charge carriers and decreases the recombination rate but also enhances the ability to absorb light. Thus, Enhancing the crystallinity is a crucial approach to improve the photocatalytic performance of materials by minimizing structural defects (H. Li et al., 2024).

The TEM analysis offers a visual representation of the structural properties of both pure Ti-TPA-MOF and its heterojunction composite, as illustrated in the images. These images provide insights into the morphology and composition of these materials, allowing for a deeper understanding of their properties and potential applications. Fig. 2(b) specifically showcases the existence of pure Ti-TPA-MOF particles. The particles exhibit a remarkably smooth surface and can be observed to have a partially columnar structure. The core-shell structure of the heterojunction composites shown in part (c) of Fig. 2 is characterized by a core made of Cu2S and a shell composed of Ti-TPA-MOF. This encapsulation process has been accomplished with success. In addition, the coating of CQDs on Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF resulted in the successful creation of a heterojunction composite, as can be observed in Fig. 2(d, e). The virgin Ti-TPA-MOF is depicted by the lighter background in the image, while the dark dots, both coarse and fine, represent the Cu2S and CQDs, respectively. The TEM images serve as concrete proof of the successful creation of the core-shell structure while also validating that the Ti-TPA-MOF's morphology remains unaltered once the nanoparticles have been encapsulated. According to Fig. 2(f-k), elemental mapping profiles from EDS analysis confirmed the presence of titanium, carbon, oxygen, copper, and sulfur in the nano-composites. The findings unambiguously demonstrated the effective integration of Cu2S nanoparticles into the Ti-TPA-MOF, resulting in the development of a core-shell structure within the Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF composite.

XPS was employed to perform a detailed investigation of the chemical valence state of surface elements within the heterojunction composite, enabling a comprehensive analysis of their composition. The attendance of various elements, including sulfur, copper, titanium, carbon, and oxygen, is noticeable in the CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF composites, as depicted in Fig. 3(a). Based on the given details, it can be observed from Fig. 3(b) that the binding energy peaks for Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 are 464.45 and 458.55 eV, correspondingly. Based on the data, it can be concluded that Ti4+ is present in the Ti-OXO cluster, as indicated by the observed values. This suggests the oxidation state of titanium within the cluster (Ahmadpour et al., 2020). Different oxygen species, including O 1S, CO, Ti-OXO cluster, and hydroxyl groups, can potentially be inferred from the binding energy peaks observed in the range of 530.35 and 531.85 eV (Guo et al., 2015). Copper's existence was confirmed by analyzing the Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 binding energy levels, which were determined to be 944.65 and 962.2 eV, as shown in Fig. 3(c) (Artchuea et al., 2022; Iqbal et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 3(d), sulfur's presence can be detected through the binding energy values found at 162.46 and 161.13 eV, representing the S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 states, correspondingly (Abbassian and Rahmani, 2024; Daskalakis et al., 2020; Lv et al., 2023).

The fabricated photocatalysts were analyzed using DRS spectra to reveal their optical absorption features, shown in Fig. 4(a). The highest absorption edge of Ti-TPA-MOF is most pronounced around 335 nm, indicating that it does not absorb any visible light wavelengths (Abbassian and Rahmani, 2024). Heterojunction composites, in stark contrast, display an impressively broad absorption spectrum spanning from 500 to 550 nm, facilitating effective utilization of visible light harvesting. The addition of CQDs and the encapsulation of Cu2S nanoparticles in the Ti-TPA-MOF framework bring about a notable change in the absorption edge of the heterojunction composite structure. The introduction of Cu2S into Ti-TPA-MOF results in a remarkable shift of the maximum absorption edge towards higher wavelengths, whereas the incorporating CQDs into Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF does not lead to a significant change in this regard. The modification highlights a prosperous collaboration between the three components, resulting in an enhanced capacity of the material to facilitate and execute photocatalytic reactions within the visible light spectrum. The remarkable alteration observed in DRS demonstrates the heightened capacity to absorb light. The optical band gap was calculated by applying Tauc's relation, using the absorption spectra data as the foundation for the calculation.

Utilizing the information presented in Fig. 4(b), it is possible to calculate the band gap by constructing a graph that displays the correlation between

The determination of the bandgap values for various materials yielded the following results: Ti-TPA-MOF exhibited a bandgap of 3.6 eV (Abbassian and Rahmani, 2024), whereas CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF displayed a band gap of 2.39 eV. The findings suggest that the CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF heterojunction composite showcases a broader absorption range and a more robust photo-response under visible light. The light-capturing capacity of the composites has been greatly improved by the incorporation of CQDs and Cu2S nanoparticles, resulting in a reduction of the band gap.

Recombination between electron-hole pairs during a photocatalytic reaction can have a substantial effect on the efficiency of the process. This recombination process results in the emission of PL and release of energy. Therefore, reducing PL intensity is a reliable measure of the photoinduced charge carriers' efficiency, indicating a more effective reaction (Liang et al., 2018; Malik et al., 2018). The characteristics of photoexcited (e-)-(h+) pairs in both pure samples and heterostructure composites were investigated by implementing PL measurements. An investigation was conducted to analyze the photoluminescence related to the core-shell structure of Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF to determine the role of CQDs. By adding CQDs, the intensity of electron-hole recombination is significantly reduced. The reason for this can be related to adding a new path for the transfer and separation of electron-holes. The PL intensity of the heterojunction composite showed a notable reduction, as depicted in Fig. 5(a), in contrast to the pristine MOF. In the pure Ti-MOF, a prominent emission peak around 320 nm is evident, indicating the direct recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes from LUMO to HOMO. This observation aligns with existing literature reports on the subject (Abbassian and Rahmani, 2024). The decrease in PL intensity suggests that there is an affective charge immigration occurring between Cu2S, MOF, and CQDs, leading to the inhibition of photogenerated carrier recombination.

It has been previously emphasized that photocarrier transport is important in photocatalytic reactions. A study conducted to examine the migration and conductivity of photoexcited carriers in the procreated samples, utilizing transient photocurrent measurement techniques. The photocurrent density-time curves of the fabricated samples can be observed in Fig. 5(b), along with their prompt transient photocurrent responses to the switching on and off light. The observation is clear that the transient optical current of CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF exhibits a distinct and rapid growth rate when subjected to lamp radiation, in contrast to pure MOF. The successful integration of CQDs and Cu2S nanoparticles in this research has enhanced photocatalytic capabilities, primarily due to the effective migration of electrons and the suppression of (e-)-(h+) pairs recombination. The photocatalytic efficiency experiences a notable enhancement with incorporating CQDs and Cu2S nanoparticles into the system. This results in a significant improvement in the overall performance of the photocatalyst. Additionally, the photocurrent strength of CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF remains consistent with repeated lamp cycles, demonstrating remarkable stableness in photoelectric chemical reactions. Additionally, it exemplifies its capacity to yield a greater quantity of electrons when exposed to light and displays superior efficiency in both the separation and transfer of electrons, outperforming Ti-TPA-MOF.

Quantum dots, also known as semiconductor nanocrystals, are ultra-small semiconductor particles ranging from 1 to less than 12 nanometers in size. Quantum mechanical effects give rise to distinct optical and electronic properties in these particles, setting them apart from larger particles (Abbassian and Rahmani, 2024; Q.-S. Si et al., 2020). The achievement of generating CQDs with a mean size of 10.67 nm is visually depicted in Fig. 6(a), and this outcome is substantiated by the DLS analysis, reinforcing the accuracy of the outcomes. The examination of DLS and nanoparticle size confirms that carbon quantum dots can be made at 180°C for 120 min. The identification of chemical characteristics and component bonding is demonstrated in Fig. 6(b) through FT-IR spectroscopy. The peaks observed in the range of 400–800 cm−1 are directly associated with the vibrations of O-Ti-O bonds. On the other hand, the peaks between 950–1350 cm−1 and 1450–1750 cm−1 are linked to the vibrations of C–O bonds and the asymmetric stretching vibration of –COOH, correspondingly. The attendance of a peak at 3425 cm−1 is evident across all spectra, signifying the O-H stretching vibration of water molecules (H2O) (Z. Liu et al., 2021). FT-IR spectroscopy revealed a significant absorption peak at 3200–3400 cm−1, indicating –OH stretching vibrations in the CQDs. This finding confirms that there are –OH groups present on the surface of the CQDs (Zhi Li et al., 2019b). Peaks corresponding to C–O, –COOH, and –OH are detected at identical wavelengths in the CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF composite material. The FT-IR spectroscopic analysis confirms the effective attachment of CQDs to Ti-TPA-MOF, as indicated by the outcomes. The BET analysis revealed a decrease in the BET of the heterojunction composites when compared to the pure Ti-MOF, primarily due to the incorporation of Cu2S nanoparticles and CQDs. The characteristics related to the texture of the catalysts are presented in Table 1. Therefore, the ability of the composites to absorb substances was slightly diminished compared to the original MOF material. Nevertheless, the composites exhibit enhanced photocatalytic degradation owing to the increased quantity of active metal sites, the incorporation of electron-conducting CQDs for efficient light capturing, and the attendance of Cu2S nanoparticles, which aid in minimizing the energy gap. As illustrated in Fig. 6(c, d), when the pressure approaches atmospheric levels, the N2 adsorption isotherms of both the Ti-TPA-MOF and heterojunction composite at 77 K exhibit type 1 behavior, indicating microporous characteristics in the synthesized photocatalysts (Jiang et al., 2021; Zhi Li et al., 2019a). The Fig. 6(e, f) displays the pore size distribution of both the pure MOF and the composite, as determined using the BJH method.

In contrast to the pristine MOF, the analysis of band positions depicted in Fig. 7 indicates that both CQDs and Cu2S exhibit elevated conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) positions. CQDs and Cu2S have been documented in the literature with different energy bandgap values. Specifically, CQDs have an energy bandgap (Eg) of 2.4 eV, while Cu2S has an Eg value of 1.43 eV. Additionally, the conduction band energy levels (ECB) for CQDs and Cu2S were approximately −0.86 and −0.78 eV/NHE, respectively. On the flip side, the valence band energy levels (EVB) for CQDs and Cu2S were found to be approximately 1.54 and 0.65 eV/NHE, respectively (Thakur et al., 2020; Q. Wang et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2023). The determination of band positions heavily relies on the essential parameter of the Eg value, which is 3.6 ev for Ti-TPA-MOF. By analyzing the electronegativity of Ti-TPA-MOF (x=5.9) and utilizing relevant formulas, one can forecast the EHOMO and ELUMO values for Ti-TPA-MOF when compared to the standard hydrogen electrode. As a result, the obtained values are 3.29 and −0.335 ev, respectively (Abbassian and Rahmani, 2024; Butler and Ginley, 1978; Hlophe and Dlamini, 2019; Shao et al., 2020; X. Yang et al., 2020).

The LUMO of Ti-TPA-MOF, being in a more positive position, serves as a suitable receptor for the photogenerated electrons found in the CB of CQDs and Cu2S, thereby aiding in their efficient transportation. The migration of HOMO holes in Ti-MOF occurs towards the CQDs and Cu2S due to their valence band being in a higher energy level and carrying a more negatively charged state. This phenomenon results from the formation of holes within the Ti-MOF structure. Implementing a new pathway has led to a substantial improvement in the overall photocatalytic performance. This enhancement is attributed to the increased lifetime of immigrated charges and the effective suppression of recombination of (e-)-(h+) pairs.

When subjected to visible light, the heterojunction composites demonstrate a distinct behavior, unlike Ti-TPA-MOF. Upon being exposed to visible light, CQDs and Cu2S demonstrate an exceptional capacity to become photoexcited, resulting in the creation of electrons and holes. This phenomenon is made possible by their unique visible-light energy gap, which enables the efficient conversion of light energy into excited charge carriers. The light-induced electrons in nanoparticles are efficiently captured by the Ti-OXO clusters within the Ti-MOF, which consist of Ti4+. During this process, the Ti4+ changes to Ti3+ within the heterojunction structure. Superoxide radicals (•O2 -) are created through the attachment of O2 molecules to the porous Ti-MOF, leading to an electron exchange with Ti3+ ions. This exchange is facilitated by the reduction capability of Ti3+. As a result, a chemical photoreaction takes place, leading to the production of highly reactive •O2 - radicals. The oxidation process converts Ti3+ ions into Ti4+ ions, resulting in a transformation. CQDs play a vital role in collecting and transferring electrons, while Cu2S acts as a recombination inhibitor and narrows the band gap. The heterogeneous composite's constituent materials play a vital role in efficiently segregating surface electrons and holes, thereby serving as active sites for photochemical reactions and leading to the production of highly reactive species. These species possess the remarkable ability to degrade pollutants through photodegradation. Charge separation occurs when electrons that are photoexcited from the conduction bands (CBs) of CQDs and Cu2S are transferred to the LUMO of Ti-MOF. Holes within the nanoparticle valence band facilitate the process of water oxidation, resulting in the production of •OH. Contaminants can be efficiently transformed into different substances by the photo-degrading free radicals, namely •OH and •O2 -, through their robust conversion process. Additionally, it is essential to highlight that the standard redox potentials for the (O2/•O2 -) and (•OH/OH) are (-0.046) and (+2.38) ev, correspondingly (Hu et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2017; Tian et al., 2015). Ensuring that the nanoparticles' CB chemical potential is more negative than O2/•O2 - is essential for promoting the reduction of O2 molecules and the generation of •O2 - radicals. The chemical potential of the VB must be at a higher positive charge for the oxidation of H2O to •OH (+2.38 eV potential) to occur. The way photodegradation occurs can be outlined as follows. Fig. 8(a) illustrates two potential degradation pathways based on the identified compounds (Arhoutane et al., 2019; Shirazinejad et al., 2023; D. Wang et al., 2024). The MS spectrum of different species in the presence of CQDs/Cu2S @Ti-TPA-MOF heterojunction composite is depicted in Fig. 8(b-e). In Fig. 8(b), it was determined that the mass-to-charge ratio (m/z) of gentamicin is 477. However, after 30 minutes of degradation, the peak intensity of gentamicin decreased, and new product peaks with m/z values of 318, 321, 257, 258, and 229 emerged, as shown in Fig. 8(c). Upon exposure to photocatalysis for 60 minutes, several new molecular ions were detected with mass-to-charge ratios (m/z) of 114, 119, 146, 162, 174, and 177, as illustrated in Fig. 8(d). However, following 90 minutes of photocatalysis, the intensity of peaks corresponding to m/z 114 and 119 increased despite a decrease in intensity for other product peaks, as shown in Fig. 8(e). The findings showed that the byproducts formed in the process of photocatalytic degradation were diminished in a step-by-step manner.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigate the photodegradation of gentamicin across various conditions
Impact of different photocatalytic materials and photodegradation kinetics
ce:section.ce:para: The effectiveness of different catalysts in photocatalysis was determined by examining gentamicin's photodecomposition. The UV-Vis absorption spectra and degradation of gentamicin are displayed in Fig. 9(a) for various nanocomposites employing CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF, Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF, Cu2S, and Ti-TPA-MOF. These experiments were conducted at a pH level of 6.5. The Ti-MOF, Cu2S, and Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF reduced approximately 14, 42, and 64 % of gentamicin after being illuminated for 90 min. The CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF samples showed better photocatalytic degradation than Ti-MOF and Cu2S alone, suggesting their combined effect is more impactful. Among the nanocomposites prepared, the CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF exhibited the highest activity, achieving 90 % gentamicin removal under visible light for 90 min. Adding CQDs to the Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF composite increases the degradation rate from 64 % to 90 %, directly linked to CQDs integration. CQDs incorporation in the composite reduces electron-hole recombination rate, shown in the PL analysis section with decreased intensity and recombination. CQDs introduction creates new pathways for electron and hole migration, improving photodecomposition efficiency as seen in Fig. 7. CQDs attached to Ti-TPA-MOF absorb and trap light, with high electron conductivity enhancing optical degradation efficiency and overall process efficiency. The photocatalytic reactions followed a pseudo-first order reaction kinetic equation, as displayed in Fig. 9(b). The photoreaction rate was determined using the apparent reaction rate constant (k) derived from the photo-degradation curves of -ln (CG/C0) plotted against the irradiation time. The results from Fig. 9(b) verify the k values of different samples, showing that the reaction rates of the samples followed this sequence: CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF > Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF > Cu2S > Ti-TPA-MOF. Specifically, the k value of CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF (0.0247 min−1) is 16.64 times higher than that of Ti-TPA-MOF (0.0015 min−1) and 4.41 times higher than that of Cu2S (0.0056 min−1). This indicates that the combination of components promoted the photocatalytic gentamicin degradation.

Fig. 9(c) depicts the photo-degradation efficiency of gentamicin at 25 mg/l and pH 6.5, as influenced by the varying dosage of CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF. As the dosage of CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF enhanced from 50 to 150 mg, the photo-degradation efficiency improved from 48 % to 90 %. However, beyond 150 mg, the photo-degradation efficiency decreased significantly to approximately 62 % when the dosage was further increased to 200 mg. Two potential factors may be responsible for the decrease in photo-degradation efficiency observed. Increasing the amount of catalyst used could lead to a rise in suspension cloudiness, potentially reducing the penetration of visible light and the effectiveness of photoactivation. The suspension may experience deactivation of photon-activated species due to collisions when there is a high concentration of CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF catalyst present in the system. Furthermore, the build-up of excess CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF could result in a decrease in its surface area that is accessible, consequently hindering the uptake of gentamicin molecules. (Abbassian and Rahmani, 2024).

The pH impact on the yield of gentamicin photodegradation was examined within the pH levels between 3.5 and 9.5. Throughout the experiment, the dosage of CQDs/Cu2s@Ti-TPA-MOF (150 mg/l) and gentamicin concentration (25 mg/l) remained constant. Fig. 9(d) shows the impact of pH on gentamicin photodegradation using core-shell composites. The study reveals that the most effective pH for maximum efficiency is 6.5. Gentamicin degradation is highest at pH 9.5 but diminishes as pH decreases from 6.5 to 3.5. This suggests that lower pH levels decrease the tendency of the composites to capture gentamicin. As the pH level rises, the adsorption capacity of composites is amplified by the electrostatic attraction between positively charged gentamicin and negatively charged components. The variation in degradation efficiency across various pH levels is due to electrostatic interactions. Optimal conditions occur when pHPZC < pH < pKa, allowing gentamicin and the photocatalyst to attract each other (Fallah et al., 2023; Ngo et al., 2023). Gentamicin is positively charged below its pKa value of 9.5, while the Ti-TPA-MOF is negatively charged beyond its point zero charge (pHPZC ∼ 5.2) (Abbassian and Rahmani, 2024; Bhembe and Dlamini, 2020; Escobar et al., 2019). It is essential to acknowledge that attaining an alkaline pH in water-based solutions demands a considerable amount of alkalizing agents, which can pose obstacles when dealing with significant amounts of wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Appraisals of mineralization performance and ESR investigation
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the catalysts' effectiveness, it is crucial to analyze their mineralization ability during photocatalytic degradation. The data presented in Fig. 10 (a) illustrates the varying effectiveness of different materials in removing TOC (Total Organic Carbon). Pure Ti-TPA-MOF, Cu2S, and Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF were found to remove 8.67 %, 45.83 %, and 54.70 % of TOC, respectively. Nevertheless, the elimination efficiency of TOC achieved 68.84 % during a 90-minute period when the CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF heterojunction composites were present. The findings demonstrate that the CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF heterojunction composites exhibited significantly greater mineralization efficiency in gentamicin photodegradation than other photocatalysts. The findings outlined previously demonstrate that the process of photocatalytic degradation is successful in breaking down gentamicin molecules into carbon dioxide, water, and several smaller intermediate compounds.

To identify the active radical species participating in the photocatalytic decomposition of gentamicin, experiments were carried out using KI, IPA, and BNQ to capture different radicals (h+, •OH–, and •O2 -) (Abbassian and Rahmani, 2024; L. Yue et al., 2021). It is evident from Fig. 10 (b) that the photocatalytic activity was significantly altered by including scavengers. The inclusion of KI, IPA, and BNQ caused a reduction in the photodecomposition efficiency by 85.63 %, 41.76 %, and 26.33 %, respectively, when compared to the experiment conducted without a scavenger. This suggests that all scavengers could reduce the photodecomposition efficiency when exposed to the CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF photocatalytic composite. Nevertheless, BNQ and IPA were discovered to cause a substantial negative impact on the photodecomposition performance, underscoring the significance of •OH– and •O2 - within the mechanism. An ESR analysis was performed on the CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF composite to verify the presence of the two reactive species, with the findings in Fig. 10 (c, d). Neither of the reactive species displayed any peaks when Ti-TPA-MOF was present. Nevertheless, the CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF led to the identification of peaks that matched with •OH– and •O2 - in the form of DMPO adducts. The reactivity of •OH– and •O2 - radicals in photodegradation processes has been substantiated by evidence, particularly in the attendance of heterojunction composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Durableness, reusability, and interference impact assessment
ce:section.ce:para: Ensuring photocatalytic stability and recyclability is of paramount importance in practical applications. The effectiveness of the CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF photocatalytic composites in degrading gentamicin under visible light was assessed through endurance experiments, focusing on their reusability. In Fig. 11 (a), it can be observed that the degradation of gentamicin in the recycling evaluation of photocatalytic heterojunction composites displayed consistency over five cycles, with only a slight 5 % decline in photocatalytic efficiency. These findings underscore the significance of sustaining stability in photocatalysts within aqueous reaction environments for efficient photocatalytic performance. The sturdy core-shell structures of the composites allow the embedded nanoparticles to uphold their effective photocatalytic capabilities and optical properties over multiple uses. The crystalline phase of the heterojunction composites was evaluated through XRD analysis after usage, and its comparison was made with the initial samples. Fig. 11 (b) demonstrates that aside from a few peaks associated with Ti-TPA-MOF, the prominent diffraction peaks of composites remained intact, affirming the structural integrity of the photocatalysts even after repeated cycles of photocatalytic reactions.

The research focused on contaminants with a higher pKa value than the photocatalyst's pzc, including azithromycin (pKa ≈ 8.5)(Kong et al., 2017), phenol (pKa = 9.5), and acetaminophen (pKa = 9.38) (Fallah et al., 2023). The photocatalyst and pollutant are attracted to each other when the pH levels fall between the pHPZC and pKa, due to having opposite charges (Fallah et al., 2023). After 90 minutes, degradation efficiency decreased to 58.9 %, 54.8 %, and 78.8 % for phenol, acetaminophen, and azithromycin, respectively. Outcomes are indicated in Fig. 11 (c). Photodegradation efficiency is influenced by factors like pollutant size, surface charge, and reactivity with free radicals. The degradation efficiency is decreased by the presence of phenol and acetaminophen, as their smaller size and rapid reaction with hydroxyl radicals hinders the process (Mustafa and Oladipo, 2021).

The cost of each system was further evaluated for a 1 L reactor under optimized conditions using 60 W visible light by estimating the electrical energy consumed per order (EEO). The Ti-TPA-MOF, Cu2S, Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF, and new heterojunction composites have varying energy requirements based on their rate constants. Referring to Fig. 11 (d), the total energy needed for each material is 1374.2, 380.4, 202.84, and 88.03 kWh m3, respectively. In terms of cost, this translates to $218.8, $63.15, $33.67, and $14.61 per m3 of gentamicin degradation, respectively, assuming an electricity price of $0.166 per kWh in the U.S. The heterojunction composite system yielded the lowest electrical energy per order and the highest efficiency in gentamicin degradation. Eq. (11) was utilized to compute the amount of electrical energy required per order to decrease the concentration of gentamicin (Abbassian and Rahmani, 2024).

, respectively (Abbassian and Rahmani, 2024).

A solvothermal method was utilized to successfully synthesize innovative heterojunction photocatalysts of CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF. This process involved merging CQDs with a Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF composite, leading to innovative photocatalytic materials with enhanced properties. These photocatalysts were then evaluated for their photoactivity in the photodecomposition of gentamicin under visible light exposure. In contrast to the pristine Ti-MOF, the photocatalyst CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF displays many advantages. It possesses more active sites, facilitates a more efficient separation of (e-)-(h+) pairs, effectively suppresses recombination, and has a narrower band gap. The photocatalytic performance of CQDs/Cu2S@Ti-TPA-MOF exceeded that of other photocatalysts produced in the gentamicin decomposition, showcasing its superior efficiency. The integration of Cu2S into Ti-MOF and its combination with CQDs has showcased its capability as a viable technique for wastewater treatment. Using material advantages, this method not only improves the durability of semiconductors but also yields promising outcomes in the purification procedure.
Khalil Abbassian: Writing – review & editing. Mohammad Rahmani: Writing – review & editing.
